haunted_house_information.hours_of_operation: Seasonal (September 27 through November 2). Fridays and Saturdays: 7:00 PM – 12:30 AM; Thursdays and Sundays: 7:30 PM – 11:00 PM. We open ticketing and parking lot one hour before listed opening times, and the last group typically enters the haunt 15–20 minutes before closing, depending on crowd size and safety staffing. We are closed Monday–Wednesday except for one private-corporate buyout night and one “low-scare” charity night, both by reservation only. We also run abbreviated hours (7:00 PM – 10:00 PM) on Halloween night if it falls on a weekday, but for this policy period we are planning for a Friday Halloween-style schedule with extended hours the weekend before October 31.
haunted_house_information.event_description:
Nightfall Frights Haunted Warehouse is a seasonal, walk-through haunted attraction built inside a converted 38,000-square-foot former distribution warehouse in an industrial park on the west side of Columbus, Ohio. We operate as a professional, ticketed haunted house experience during the fall Halloween season, focusing on immersive sets, theatrical-level lighting and sound, and trained scare actors, while maintaining a strong focus on guest safety and crowd management.

LAYOUT AND EXPERIENCE: The attraction is laid out as a single, continuous walkthrough that takes most guests between 18 and 25 minutes to complete, depending on their walking speed and crowd flow. The queue line begins outside under a covered loading-dock canopy with metal crowd-control barricades, rope stanchions, and clearly marked entrance and exit points. Guests enter in groups of 6–10 people at a time to avoid overcrowding inside the scenes. The interior is divided into themed zones, including a decrepit Victorian manor, an abandoned medical research wing, a blackout maze with controlled lighting, and a final industrial “nightmare factory” scene that uses fog machines, strobe lighting, and sound effects. All pathways are at least 42 inches wide to accommodate most mobility devices, and emergency exits are placed approximately every 75–100 feet, clearly marked with lit signage and unobstructed paths.

STAFFING AND TRAINING: We employ a mix of paid seasonal staff and a small number of long-term part-time employees. On a typical weekend night, we have approximately 35–45 actors in costume and makeup, 8–10 front-of-house staff (ticketing, security check, queue management), and 6–8 operations staff (safety spotters, maintenance, and supervisors). All actors and staff are required to attend a mandatory safety orientation before the season begins, which covers topics such as no physical contact with guests, how to safely navigate in low-light conditions, emergency evacuation procedures, handling guests who panic or wish to exit early, and protocols for reporting any hazards or injuries immediately. We also hold a short pre-show meeting each operating night to review crowd expectations, weather considerations, and any incident reports from prior nights.

SAFETY AND ACCESSIBILITY: The haunt is designed to be intense but physically safe. There is no intentional contact between actors and guests; our rule, which is posted on signage and announced verbally, is "You don’t touch them, and they don’t touch you." We do not use real weapons; any prop items (foam axes, plastic knives, etc.) are inspected for sharp edges and are clearly theatrical. Floor surfaces are leveled plywood with anti-slip paint, and any elevation changes (small ramps or steps) are either removed or clearly marked with reflective tape and additional lighting. We do not use open flames; all candles, lanterns, and torches are LED-based. Fog machines use water-based, non-toxic fog fluid, and we have signage at the entrance advising guests with asthma or respiratory sensitivities. Strobe lights are used in limited, clearly contained sections, and we post a photosensitivity warning at ticketing and on our website.

We have a first-aid station near the exit staffed by at least one person per night who has current CPR/First Aid certification, and we maintain a stocked first-aid kit, AED, and clearly posted emergency contact numbers. We coordinate with local police and fire departments prior to opening each season; the fire marshal inspects our layout, exit signage, and electrical setup before we open to the public. We maintain clear fire lanes in the parking lot and do not allow guests to line up in any vehicle traffic areas.

OPERATIONS AND TICKETING: Tickets are sold both online and at an on-site box office. Time-slot ticketing is used on peak nights to spread attendance and reduce excessive queue times. We use handheld scanners to validate tickets and track entry counts. Wristbands or hand stamps are used on special event nights (such as the low-scare charity night) to designate guests who have purchased add-ons or special access. We have written policies against intoxicated or disruptive guests; we reserve the right to refuse entry or remove guests for safety reasons, and we coordinate with an off-duty police officer who is present on peak nights to assist with security issues.

SPECIAL NIGHTS AND VARIATIONS: During the season we host one "Lights-On, No-Scare" family preview afternoon, where the house is fully lit, sound is at low volume, and no actors jump out or aggressively scare guests. This is marketed toward families with younger children and individuals with sensory sensitivities. We also host one private corporate team-building buyout, where the experience is the same as a regular night but the group has exclusive access by pre-arrangement.

In summary, Nightfall Frights Haunted Warehouse is a professionally run, fully indoor, walk-through haunted house attraction with a strong emphasis on theatrical presentation and guest safety. There are no mechanical rides, no hayrides, no bungee or aerial elements, and no high-speed or moving-vehicle components. Our primary exposures are typical for a commercial haunted house: slip/trip/fall potential, low-light navigation, and emotional distress or startle reactions, all of which we mitigate through design, training, and supervision.
